MU.headline: Two-quarter beat-and-raise cadence accelerating — Q2 revenue beat guide by 28%, EPS by 45%; Q3 guide implies further +40% QoQ.

shifts.note: QoQ growth re-accelerated from +21% to +75% and is guided to +40% again
shifts.trend: Revenue $13.6B → $23.9B actual → $33.5B guide (YoY: +57% → +196% → implied +250%+)

shifts.note: Pricing power compounding — AI demand outpaces industry supply
shifts.trend: Non-GAAP GM 56.8% → 74.9% → ~81% guide (≈ +24 pts of expansion in two quarters)

shifts.note: Beat magnitude widening, not narrowing — credibility on the raised Q3 guide is high
shifts.trend: Q2 revenue guided $18.7B → printed $23.9B (+28% beat); Q3 now guided to $33.5B (+40% QoQ)

shifts.note: Same constraint language for two consecutive quarters; tightness is persistent, not transient
shifts.trend: 'Supply allocation decisions may impact certain customers' (Q1) → 'Industry supply allocation decisions continue' (Q2) — no easing language added

shifts.note: HBM hyperscaler reliance increasing — single-customer dependency to monitor
shifts.trend: One customer reached 13% of six-month revenue (Q2 disclosure) — primarily CMBU (HBM/hyperscale)

shifts.note: Singapore HBM advanced-packaging fab broke ground; capacity additions from CY2027
shifts.trend: FY26 capex ~$20B (Q1 guide); Q1 capex $5.39B + Q2 capex $5.0B = $10.4B run-rate H1 — on pace for $20B+

shifts.note: First meaningful dividend hike in years; mgmt signaling cycle durability
shifts.trend: Dividend $0.115/share (Q1) → $0.15/share (Q2, +30%)

MU.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening — beat-and-raise cadence is widening, gross-margin expansion is compounding, and guidance credibility is high. The only emerging risk is HBM customer concentration (one customer at 13% of revenue). Supply tightness language has not eased — bullish for memory pricing through CY2026.

NVDA.headline: DC growth holding ~+66–68% YoY; customer concentration formally disclosed at 4-way >10%; China is now effectively zero, but supply/energy commentary unchanged.

shifts.note: Slight re-acceleration on a full-year basis after Blackwell ramp
shifts.trend: DC YoY +66% (Q3 FY26) → +68% (FY26 full year); Networking +162% on GB200/GB300 NVLink ramp

shifts.note: Hyperscaler dependence newly visible — diligence risk-additive
shifts.trend: Q3 disclosed 4 direct customers each >10% of revenue (22/15/13/11%); 10-K did not previously break this out

shifts.note: China optionality fully impaired across both filings
shifts.trend: Q3 China $3.0B vs $8.1B prior-year Q3; H20 effectively zero by FY26 after $4.5B Q1 inventory charge; Feb 2026 H200 license carries 25% US import tariff
Non-GAAP GM 73.4% (Q3) → 73.5% guide (Q1 FY27); compressed YoY on Blackwell full-system mix but holding within target

shifts.note: Quote nearly identical across the two filings
shifts.trend: Both filings cite data-center / energy / capital availability as the gating constraint on customer deployments — no easing language added

shifts.note: Vendor-financing footprint growing — watch FCF translation
shifts.trend: FY26 10-K disclosed $17.5B in private/infra investments plus $3.5B land/power/shell guarantees to early-stage AI customers; Q3 added $7.5B new DC lease obligations FY26 Q4–FY30

NVDA.verdict: Story still about supply-constrained AI capex. The new disclosures (4-way customer concentration, vendor-financing footprint) are risk-additive but don't break the thesis; China optionality is now fully impaired and should be modeled at zero.

AMAT.headline: HBM-driven DRAM mix rising sharply while China revenue and logic foundry spending slide; a $253M BIS settlement one-time charge masked a stable underlying margin.

shifts.trend: DRAM share of Semiconductor Systems: 26% (FY25 annual) → 34% (Q1 FY26) +8pts, driven by HBM technology-transition capex at memory customers
shifts.note: Foundry/logic fell from 67% to 62% as trailing-edge logic demand remains weak

shifts.trend: China revenue $8.5B (30% of FY25, down from 37% FY24) → -7% YoY in Q1 FY26; Taiwan surged to 24% FY25 then +46% in Q1 FY26
shifts.note: $253M BIS export-controls compliance settlement in Q1 FY26 adds legal tail-risk on top of the structural mix shift away from China

shifts.trend: Gross margin 48.7% (FY25) → 49.0% (Q1 FY26); operating margin fell 4.3pts in Q1 to 26.1% but entirely from the one-time BIS settlement charge
shifts.note: Underlying GM stable; AGS services +15% adding recurring-revenue cushion

shifts.trend: Revenue +4% FY25 → -2% YoY in Q1 FY26 on weaker foundry/logic and Korea (SK Hynix timing)
shifts.note: Memory capex offsets are real but not yet large enough to replace the foundry volume decline

AMAT.verdict: Thesis intact on HBM/advanced packaging as the demand driver, but China BIS exposure (settlement + ongoing mix shift) is a real and recurring risk. Revenue growth is negative YoY in Q1 — recovery depends on whether HBM capex ramp outpaces logic weakness in H2 FY26.

AMD.headline: Data Center growth re-accelerated from +32% FY25 to +57% Q1 FY26 on MI350 GPU and EPYC; gross margin expanding 3pts as DC mix dominates; China trajectory improving after MI308 write-down cycle.

shifts.trend: Data Center revenue: $16.6B (+32% FY25) → $5.8B in Q1 FY26 (+57% YoY); segment now 56% of Q1 revenue vs 48% for full FY25
shifts.note: OpenAI 6 GW multi-year GPU purchase agreement (MI450, announced Oct 2025) provides long-cycle revenue visibility not yet in reported numbers

shifts.trend: Gross margin 50% (FY25) → 53% (Q1 FY26, +3pts) on Data Center segment mix shift; DC segment operating margin 28% in Q1 vs 22% FY25 full-year
shifts.note: FY25 DC margin was depressed by $440M net MI308 China export charges; Q1 FY26 is a cleaner read

shifts.trend: $800M MI308 inventory charge Q2 FY25 → $360M reversal Q4 FY25 → some MI325 China licenses granted Feb 2026 (25% US re-import tariff applies)
shifts.note: China remains 22% of FY25 revenue; licensing with tariff is better than zero but partial

shifts.trend: No single customer >10% in FY25 → international now 74% of Q1 FY26 revenue (vs 66% prior year), reflecting hyperscaler GPU shipping concentration
shifts.note: OpenAI warrant (160M shares) creates aligned incentives but also dependency on one strategic partner

AMD.verdict: Thesis strengthening — DC re-acceleration is real and the OpenAI anchor deal de-risks the multi-year demand outlook. China trajectory has turned from write-down to incremental license recovery. The key remaining question is MI350/MI450 supply cadence vs NVIDIA Blackwell.

AMKR.headline: Revenue inflected from +6% FY25 to +27.5% Q1 26 as all four end markets grew; capex step-up from $905M to $2.5-3.0B FY26 guide is the defining signal — Arizona advanced-packaging bet now fully committed.

shifts.trend: Net sales: $6.71B (+6% FY25) → $1.68B (+27.5% YoY Q1 26); all four end markets positive in Q1 for first time in multiple quarters
shifts.note: Computing end market specifically called out as driven by datacenter (HBM/2.5D/HPC packaging)

shifts.trend: Capex: $904.6M (FY25, 13.5% of sales) → guided $2.5-3.0B FY26 (~37-45% of FY25 sales), reaffirmed in Q1
shifts.note: Arizona Facility (1.8M sq ft, manufacturing begins H1 2028) + $407M CHIPS Act grant anchoring US expansion

shifts.trend: Gross margin 14.0% (FY25) → 14.2% (Q1 26, +2.3pts YoY) on higher factory utilization; operating margin 7.0% FY25
shifts.note: Vietnam ramp pressure that weighed on FY25 appears to be easing

shifts.trend: Apple 29.8% and Qualcomm 11.1% of FY25 sales; top-10 = 72% — unchanged disclosure in Q1
shifts.note: Concentration is a structural feature of OSAT business, not a new risk

AMKR.verdict: The capex commitment is the story — $2.5-3.0B FY26 vs $905M FY25 is a 3x step-up that signals management conviction in the HBM/AI packaging demand cycle. Revenue inflection in Q1 validates the bet. Key risk is whether Arizona comes online on schedule and at cost.

AMZN.headline: AWS re-accelerated from +20% FY25 to +28% Q1 26; capex annualizing at ~$170B+ vs $128B in FY25; RPO jumped from $244B to $364B on OpenAI $100B expansion and Anthropic commitments.

shifts.trend: AWS revenue: $128.7B (+20% FY25) → $37.6B (+28% YoY Q1 26); Cloud RPO $244B at Dec 31 → $364B at Mar 31 (+49% in one quarter)
shifts.note: OpenAI announced $100B expansion of existing $38B AWS commitment in Q1; Trainium obligations included

shifts.trend: Cash capex $77.7B (FY24) → $128.3B (FY25) → $43.2B in Q1 26 alone (annualizing >$170B)
shifts.note: Mgmt stated at FY25 that 2026 capex would 'increase further'; Q1 trajectory confirms

shifts.trend: AWS operating margin 39.4% (FY25) → 37.7% (Q1 26, -1.7pts) as infrastructure costs front-run revenue recognition
shifts.note: Margin compression is investment-led, not demand-led; RPO trajectory argues for re-expansion

shifts.trend: OpenAI $138B committed ($38B + $100B) + Anthropic $50B committed; two AI-native customers dominate the AWS RPO expansion
shifts.note: $15B invested in OpenAI Series C Preferred + committed $35B more; strategic not just commercial

AMZN.verdict: Thesis intact and accelerating — AWS is the cleanest AI infrastructure demand signal of any hyperscaler, with RPO growing faster than revenue. The margin dip is transient cost front-loading. Only risk is OpenAI/Anthropic concentration in the RPO base.

ANET.headline: Revenue growth accelerated from +28.6% to +35.1%; deferred revenue swelling from $5.37B to $6.20B signals AI Ethernet trials accumulating faster than recognizing; gross margin under pressure from memory supply tightness.

shifts.trend: Revenue: $9.0B (+28.6% FY25) → $2.71B (+35.1% YoY Q1 FY26); total RPO $7.7B at Q1 end vs $6.1B FY25-year-end
shifts.note: Americas mix rose to 84.5% from 79.7% — US AI/Cloud titan concentration intensifying

shifts.trend: Memory and silicon supply tightness first flagged as GM risk in FY25 10-K; Q1 FY26 10-Q explicitly cites it as the driver of the 180bps GM decline to 61.9%
shifts.note: ANET is procuring ahead and flagging shipment-cadence discipline — supply constraint is gating revenue recognition timing

shifts.trend: Two end customers 26% and 16% in FY25 (disclosed in 10-K); Q1 Americas jumped to 84.5% of revenue — same two customers dominating
shifts.note: Deferred revenue build ($826M in one quarter) makes the actual customer-level exposure larger than recognized revenue suggests

shifts.trend: Gross margin: 64.1% (FY25) → 61.9% (Q1 FY26, -220bps) on large-customer mix and memory cost pressure
shifts.note: R&D +24% in FY25; operating leverage intact but GM compression is a real headwind

ANET.verdict: Demand is clearly robust — deferred and binding backlog building faster than shipments. The GM compression from memory supply is the one new risk; if it persists into Q2/Q3, it will become a thesis-modifier for margin expansion. Story intact for now.

AVGO.headline: Semiconductor solutions revenue re-accelerated to +52% Q1 FY26 from +22% FY25; top-5 customer concentration jumped from ~40% to ~50% and one distributor hit 42% — XPU revenue increasingly concentrated in one hyperscaler.

shifts.trend: Semi solutions: $36.9B (+22% FY25) → $12.5B (+52% YoY Q1 FY26); total revenue +24% FY25 → +29% Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Infrastructure software +1% in Q1 (flat) as AI semi dominates the growth story

shifts.trend: Top-5 end customers: ~40% of FY25 revenue → ~50% of Q1 FY26; one semi distributor: 32% (FY25) → 42% (Q1 FY26)
shifts.note: The distributor concentration implies one hyperscaler XPU customer is effectively the majority of the Q1 semi print

shifts.trend: Gross margin 68% in both FY25 and Q1 FY26; operating margin 40% (FY25) → 44% (Q1 FY26) on lower VMware restructuring tail
shifts.note: Mgmt explicitly warned AI rack/system leasing model will expand operating margin but compress gross margin — watch for this in future quarters

shifts.trend: China revenue 20% FY24 → 17% FY25; no Q1 FY26 geographic detail, but China end-customer exposure noted as substantially lower than shipment-destination share
shifts.note: Export control risk is asymmetric: leakage via third-country routing vs direct exposure

AVGO.verdict: Thesis intact and the semi re-acceleration is real. The concentration jump (one distributor at 42%) is the most important new data point — it makes the thesis entirely dependent on one hyperscaler's XPU capex cycle continuing. Worth watching each quarter.

CAT.headline: Power & Energy backlog doubled to $51.2B at YE25 and kept growing in Q1; Power Generation sustaining +22-32% growth on data-center reciprocating engines; tariffs are a new $2.2-2.4B FY26 headwind offsetting volume gains.

shifts.trend: Power & Energy backlog $30.0B (YE24) → $51.2B (YE25) → continued growth in Q1 26; $19.3B of FY25-end backlog not expected to fill in 2026
shifts.note: Both filings cite prime power orders from data center customers seeking alternative power solutions as a new and growing demand category

shifts.trend: Power Generation sub-segment: +32% FY25 → P&E segment +22% in Q1 26; data center demand explicitly cited both periods
shifts.note: Overall Caterpillar revenues +20% FY25 → +22% Q1 26 — growth holding

shifts.trend: P&E profit margin 19.9% (FY25) → segment operating margin pressured in Q1 26 by tariff costs; Electrical Americas margin -480bps on commodity/tariff in Q1
shifts.note: Q2 FY26 tariff costs guided at ~$700M; full-year $2.2-2.4B — new and material headwind not present in FY25 guidance

shifts.trend: FY26 guidance low double-digit sales growth maintained across both filings; tariff cost range $2.2-2.4B added in both (10-K initial, confirmed Q1)
shifts.note: No guidance revision despite Q1 tariff clarity — mgmt absorbing $700M Q2 tariff without cutting the growth outlook

CAT.verdict: Data-center-driven Power Generation demand is durable — backlog doubling and prime power orders now explicitly called out. Tariffs are a real margin headwind but haven't forced a guidance cut. Story intact; margin compression is the main watch item.

CDNS.headline: Revenue growth accelerated from +14% to +19%; China revenue growing at +36% despite BIS headline noise; backlog inflected from $7.8B to $8.0B; EDA demand from AI chip design cycles is compounding.

shifts.note: Other Americas +87%, Japan +36%, Other Asia +23% in Q1 — geographically broad acceleration driven by AI chip design activity

shifts.trend: China revenue: $680M (+19% in FY25, 13% of mix) → $189.4M (+36% in Q1 26, still 13% of mix); BIS 'is-informed' letter rescinded July 2025
shifts.note: Company expects 'limited' impact from current expanded trade controls; Sep 2025 50%-affiliate rule is a risk but not yet quantified

shifts.trend: Operating margin 28% (FY25, including $128.5M BIS/DOJ settlement) → 29% (Q1 FY26); FY25 ex-settlement ≈ 30% normalized
shifts.note: Recurring revenue 77% of Q1 mix (down from 82% FY25 as hardware mix grew) — minor mix shift, not a structural concern

shifts.trend: FY25 BIS enforcement settlement $128.5M paid; BIS 'is-informed' letter for EDA exports to China imposed May 2025, rescinded July 2025
shifts.note: Regulatory tail costs appear to be winding down; Sep 2025 50%-affiliate rule is the open overhang

CDNS.verdict: Clean acceleration story with China growing faster than the company average despite export control noise. BIS settlement cost is behind them. Backlog growing. EDA is a direct proxy for AI chip design cycles — thesis intact and improving.

CEG.headline: Calpine closed ($22B, Jan 2026) making Constellation the world's largest private power producer at 55 GW; TMI/Crane DOE $1B loan guarantee secured; Meta 20-yr PPA announced Q3 now bookended by Microsoft 20-yr Crane PPA — AI hyperscaler PPA backlog building.

shifts.trend: Pre-Calpine: 32 GW nuclear/gas, $23.6B revenue FY24 → post-Calpine: 55 GW (+72%), $25.5B revenue FY25 (partial Calpine contribution only 7 days)
shifts.note: Full-year Calpine revenue will be a material uplift in FY26 — not yet in either filing

shifts.trend: Q3 FY25: 20-yr PPA with Meta (Clinton Clean Energy Center) signed; FY25 10-K: Microsoft 20-yr PPA (Crane/TMI); second AI hyperscaler anchor signed between filings
shifts.note: 2.5M retail accounts including 75% of Fortune 100 post-Calpine provide diversification beyond hyperscaler PPAs

shifts.trend: Q3: Calpine awaiting DOJ delay agreement; FY25: DOJ cleared, FERC cleared, PUCT cleared, NYPSC cleared — all approvals received; DOE $1B Crane loan guarantee at +37.5bps secured
shifts.note: NY PSC approved 20-yr ZEC extension through 2049 for nuclear fleet — removes a long-term regulatory overhang

shifts.trend: Adjusted operating EPS $3.04 Q3 FY25 → $9.39 FY25 full year; GAAP net income lower both periods on nuclear PTC headwind (timing vs FY24)
shifts.note: Nuclear PTC benefit expected to resume in FY26 as plants cycle through compliance periods

CEG.verdict: Thesis materially strengthened: scale doubled, two AI hyperscaler 20-yr PPAs in hand, regulatory runway cleared. The open question is Calpine integration execution in FY26 — the $22B deal needs to demonstrate financial synergies.

CLS.headline: Revenue run-rate accelerated from $12.2B FY25 to $16.2B annualized pace in Q1; HPS holding at 41-42% of revenue; Enterprise end market flipped from -19% (FY25) to +101% (Q1 26) on next-gen AI/ML compute ramp.

shifts.trend: Revenue: $12.2B (FY25, +annualized) → $4.05B Q1 FY26 (+53% YoY); CCS +42% FY25 → +76% Q1 26
shifts.note: Enterprise +101% Q1 26 after -19% FY25 — the hyperscaler AI/ML compute program technology transition completed and next-gen ramp has begun

shifts.trend: HPS revenue: $5.0B (41% of FY25) → ~$1.7B (42% of Q1 26); Communications end market +81% FY25 → +69% Q1 26 (still fast); CCS 70% → 80% of revenue mix
shifts.note: HPS growing slightly below CCS pace in Q1 but accelerating in absolute dollars

shifts.trend: CCS segment margin: 8.4% (Q4 FY25) → 8.6% (Q1 FY26); ATS margin 6.0%; adjusted EPS beat high end of guide
shifts.note: Revolver upsized to $1.75B — financial capacity scaling with the revenue opportunity

shifts.trend: 3 CCS customers each >10% of revenue (32%, 14%, 12%) in FY25; top-10 = 79%; Q1 confirms same hyperscaler concentration
shifts.note: Enterprise (hyperscaler AI/ML) resuming — concentration not easing, but that customer is now growing vs in a pause

CLS.verdict: Execution is clean: beat the Q1 guide, Enterprise ramp confirmed, margins expanding. The story is now entirely about hyperscaler AI/ML infrastructure demand — CLS is the most direct pure-play EMS exposure to that cycle. No new risks surfaced.

CMI.headline: Power Systems is the story: EBITDA margin expanded from 18.4% (FY24) to 22.7% (FY25) to 29.5% (Q1 FY26) in three steps; data-center-driven demand explicitly extends 6-8 quarters out with capacity ramp ongoing.

shifts.trend: Power Systems EBITDA margin: 18.4% (FY24) → 22.7% (FY25) → 29.5% (Q1 FY26), +11.1pts in 5 quarters
shifts.note: Volume leverage is compounding as data center demand fills capacity at structurally higher margins

shifts.trend: Power Systems sales: +16% FY25 ($7.46B) → +19% Q1 FY26 ($1.96B); Power Generation product line: +19% FY25 → +28% Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Data center backlog 6-8 quarters cited in FY25 10-K; Q1 maintains the same language — no shortening of visibility

shifts.trend: Engine segment (truck) dragging consolidated results (-$179M EBITDA Q1 YoY) while Power Systems more than compensates; Distribution +$68M
shifts.note: PACCAR at 13% of consolidated sales — truck weakness is a real offset to Power Systems strength

shifts.trend: FY25 10-K: 'strong demand for data center products extending out six to eight quarters'; Q1 FY26 maintains positive demand language with no sign of order book softening

CMI.verdict: Power Systems margin expansion is the cleanest operating leverage story across the power infrastructure space — three consecutive periods of margin expansion, each step driven by data center volume. Truck weakness is the only offset; watch Engine segment for the net consolidation drag.

COHR.headline: D&C segment accelerated from +34% to +41% and gross margin expanded 140bps then 243bps — both trends improving sequentially; 'scale across' interconnect added as a third growth vector alongside DCI and traditional telecom in Q3.

shifts.trend: D&C revenue: +34% YoY (Q2, $1.21B) → +41% YoY (Q3, $1.36B); total revenue +17% → +21%
shifts.note: Portfolio divestitures (aerospace Sep 25, Munich Jan 26) structurally shifting mix toward D&C; Q3 is a cleaner read

shifts.trend: Gross margin: 37% (+140bps YoY in Q2) → 38% (+243bps YoY in Q3); D&C segment profit: +44% Q2 → +49% Q3
shifts.note: Yield improvements and pricing optimization in transceivers cited both quarters; trend is accelerating, not one-time

shifts.trend: Q2 called out AI Datacenter + DCI + traditional telecom; Q3 added 'scale across' (inter-datacenter interconnect) as a fourth named category
shifts.note: New naming of scale-across is significant — signals a market forming around inter-DC fabric for AI workloads

shifts.trend: Interest expense: -$13M Q3, -$39M YTD on term-loan paydown; effective tax rate 4% YTD (Q1-Q3) on German tax changes and SBC windfalls
shifts.note: Divestitures generating cash to pay down debt — financial risk declining alongside margin expansion

COHR.verdict: Two consecutive quarters of accelerating D&C growth and expanding margins with a new demand driver (scale-across) just named in Q3 — the trajectory is clean. Thesis intact and improving. Watch for whether scale-across becomes a quantified revenue line in future quarters.

CRM.headline: Full-year FY26 reveals RPO recovered to $72.4B after the Q3 dip to $59.5B; cRPO growth (+16%) is accelerating relative to total RPO (+14%) — near-term pipeline building; Agentforce and Informatica are now live, not just announced.

shifts.trend: Revenue: +9% Q3 FY26 → +10% FY26 full year (stable); operating margin 19% Q3 → 20% FY26 (slight expansion)
shifts.note: Agentforce 360 Platform/Slack +19% is the only double-digit segment; rest of portfolio 2-9%

shifts.trend: RPO: $59.5B (Oct 31, 2025) → $72.4B (Jan 31, 2026) — recovered $13B in one quarter; cRPO +16% outpacing total RPO +14%
shifts.note: Q3 RPO dip was likely seasonal/timing; FY26 full-year RPO confirms demand pipeline intact

shifts.trend: $12.7B stock repurchased + $1.6B dividends in FY26; operating cash flow $15.0B (+15%)
shifts.note: Post-Informatica, capital allocation confirms mgmt confidence in FCF durability

shifts.trend: Informatica closed Nov 2025, contributing $0.4B revenue in FY26; Agentforce rebranding completed Q3 FY26 — unified AI/CRM stack now commercially available
shifts.note: Monetization of Agentforce/Data Cloud still 'relatively new and uncertain' per own disclosure — consumption pricing model risk

CRM.verdict: Steady execution: RPO dip resolved, margins expanding, Informatica integrated. The open question is whether Agentforce drives re-acceleration to 12-15%+ revenue growth or stays in the 10% band. At 10%, the thesis is intact but not compelling. Watch cRPO in Q1 FY27.

CRWV.headline: Revenue +112% Q1 vs +168% FY25 (moderation, but $2.08B quarterly run-rate vs $5.1B full-year is itself +63%); Microsoft concentration fell from 67% to 45% as Meta emerged at 20%; RPO surged from $60.7B to $98.8B.

shifts.trend: Quarterly revenue: $5.1B FY25 → $2.08B in Q1 26 ($8.3B annualized, +63% vs FY25 annual run-rate); RPO $60.7B (Dec 31) → $98.8B (Mar 31)
shifts.note: Growth rate YoY is slowing (168% → 112%) but absolute revenue and RPO momentum is accelerating

shifts.trend: Microsoft: 77% (top-2 FY24) → 67% (FY25) → 45% (Q1 26); Meta: not disclosed in FY25 10-K → emerges at 20% in Q1 26
shifts.note: Customer diversification is improving structurally, but CRWV is still 65% concentrated in two hyperscalers

shifts.trend: Total debt: $8.0B (FY24-end) → $21.6B (Dec 31 2025) → additional $7.8B raised post-Q1 (converts + senior notes + equity)
shifts.note: Debt-funded growth model; effective rates 9-15% on $21.6B base; net losses widening ($1.2B FY25 → $740M Q1 26)

shifts.trend: Active power: 360 MW (FY24) → 850 MW (FY25); contracted power 3.1 GW; Q1 committed $1.2B to two new DC JV equity stakes
shifts.note: Capacity is the constraint: RPO far exceeds current delivery capacity; $98.8B RPO vs $8.3B annualized revenue = ~12 years of backlog

CRWV.verdict: Extraordinary demand visibility ($98.8B RPO) but funded by rapidly accumulating debt at high rates — the financial model requires perpetual capital access. The customer diversification from MSFT-only to a two-hyperscaler base reduces concentration risk but the leverage trajectory is the dominant risk to monitor.

CSCO.headline: Networking growth accelerated from +15% to +21% across two quarters on AI Infrastructure demand; overall revenue re-accelerated to +10%; Silicon One purchase commitments building as hyperscaler orders ramp.

shifts.trend: Total revenue: +8% (Q1 FY26) → +10% (Q2 FY26); Networking product: +15% (+$1.0B Q1) → +21% (+$1.4B Q2)
shifts.note: AI Infrastructure and Data Center Switching cited as primary drivers both quarters; Service Provider and Cloud customer led in both

shifts.trend: Silicon One purchase commitments +10% vs FYE (Q1) — explicitly tied to hyperscaler contract manufacturing capacity; Q2 confirms inventory build continues
shifts.note: Mgmt warned in Q1 that Silicon One commitments raise supply-chain exposure to obsolescence risk — trade-off of capacity-securing vs inventory risk

shifts.trend: Security -4% in Q2 on Splunk shift from on-prem to cloud subscriptions and prior-gen product declines; trend expected to persist into H2 FY26
shifts.note: Security weakness is structural (product mix transition) not cyclical; Splunk revenue model shift is a multi-quarter drag

shifts.trend: Product gross margin: -0.6pts Q1 → +0.2pts Q2; effective tax rate: 15.9% Q1 → 12.9% Q2 (positive swing); diluted EPS +31% Q2
shifts.note: Margin recovery in Q2 on productivity offset; overall margin trajectory stable despite mix shift

CSCO.verdict: AI Infrastructure networking is the engine driving re-acceleration. Security drag is real but contained. Silicon One capacity commitment is building — if hyperscaler Ethernet adoption continues, CSCO's inventory build is prescient; if it stalls, it becomes a risk. Net: thesis intact.

ETN.headline: Electrical Americas backlog jumped from $13.2B (YE25) to $14.5B (Mar 26) with organic orders +42% and book-to-bill 1.2x; Boyd Thermal $9.5B acquisition adds liquid cooling as margin-accretive adjacent; Q1 operating margin compressed 480bps from commodity/tariff inflation.

shifts.trend: Electrical Americas backlog: $13.2B (YE25, +31% YoY) → $14.5B (Mar 31 26, +44% YoY); book-to-bill 1.2x both periods; organic customer orders +42% in Q1
shifts.note: Data center remains primary demand driver — commentary identical across FY25 and Q1 26

shifts.trend: Electrical Americas: +16% FY25 → +20% Q1 FY26; Electrical Global: +9% FY25 → +21% Q1 (data center now cited in Global commentary too)
shifts.note: Total backlog $19.8B at YE25, targeting 69% delivery in next 12 months — strong revenue visibility

shifts.trend: Electrical Americas operating margin: 29.9% (FY25) → 25.6% (Q1 26, -430bps); FY25 already down 30bps from FY24 on commodity inflation
shifts.note: Commodity, tariff, and wage inflation are structural near-term headwinds; volume leverage partially offsets

shifts.trend: Boyd Thermal $9.5B acquisition (liquid cooling, chip-to-grid) announced Nov 2025; closes Q2 2026; earlier acquisitions Fibrebond + Resilient in 2025
shifts.note: Eaton is using M&A to extend from power distribution into liquid cooling — addressable market expansion, not just organic

ETN.verdict: Demand is unambiguous — backlog and order momentum accelerating. The margin compression is the key watch item: if tariff/commodity headwinds ease in H2, margins re-expand on the backlog volume; if not, the stock de-rates despite strong orders. Boyd Thermal adds long-term optionality.

FN.headline: DCI revenue nearly doubled (+90.4%) and HPC grew from $85.6M to $106.7M quarter-over-quarter — two AI-specific lines emerging as distinct drivers alongside the established telecom base; overall revenue accelerating.

shifts.note: DCI is the fastest-growing optical line — inter-datacenter fabric for AI scale-across is the likely driver

shifts.trend: Non-optical (HPC + auto): $300.3M (+61.1% Q2) → $325.6M (+51.7% Q3); HPC: $85.6M → $106.7M; Telecom: $412M (+42.5% Q3) now the largest optical segment
shifts.note: HPC disclosure is relatively new — Fabrinet is now a direct AI infrastructure manufacturing partner, not just an optical CM

shifts.trend: 2 customers >10% each (48.5% combined) in Q2 → 4 customers >10% (Q3, customer consolidation from Lumentum/NeoPhotonics, Nokia/Infinera mergers)
shifts.note: Customer consolidation is a risk factor but also means Fabrinet is serving the surviving scale players

shifts.trend: Gross margin 12.2% (Q2) → 11.9% (Q3, -30bps); operating margin 10.1% → 9.9%; 6-month operating income +43-52% despite flat-to-declining GM%
shifts.note: Fabrinet's margins are structurally thin as a CM; absolute operating income +52% matters more than GM% movement

FN.verdict: DCI emergence as a fast-growing distinct line is the most important new data from the two quarters — it signals inter-DC AI fabric demand is real and accelerating. HPC growing. Thesis intact and strengthening with better revenue diversification across AI-relevant optical segments.

GEV.headline: RPO grew from $150.2B to $163.3B sequentially (+$13B in one quarter); Gas Power EBITDA margin jumped from 12.5% (FY25) to 16.3% (Q1 26) on volume and pricing; heavy-duty turbine order pace maintaining 2025's record level.

shifts.trend: Total RPO: $150.2B (YE25, +26% YoY) → $163.3B (Mar 31 26, +32% YoY); equipment RPO: $64.2B → $75.9B in one quarter
shifts.note: RPO growth outpacing revenue growth — backlog extending, not drawing down

shifts.trend: Gas Power turbine orders: 68 units FY24 → 110 units FY25 → 28 units Q1 26 (>100/yr pace); HA-Turbine orders 43 FY25 → 12 in Q1 26
shifts.note: Q1 revenues $9.3B (+16% YoY); Gas Power Q1 revenue $4.07B (+13%) as delivery of ordered backlog accelerates

shifts.trend: Gas Power EBITDA margin: 12.5% (FY25) → 16.3% (Q1 26); Power segment EBITDA margin 14.7% (FY25) → 16.3% (Q1 26) on favorable pricing and higher volume
shifts.note: Free cash flow $4.8B in Q1 26 vs $3.7B full-year FY25 — margin expansion flowing to cash

shifts.trend: Prolec GE 100% acquisition closed Feb 2026 ($5.3B for remaining 50%); $5B cumulative R&D 2025-2028 committed
shifts.note: Prolec GE adds transformer manufacturing at scale — relevant to AI data center grid connections

GEV.verdict: Both filings tell the same story: demand is unprecedented (110 HD turbines in 2025), RPO keeps growing, and margins are expanding. GEV is the cleanest proxy for the power-generation bottleneck in the AI buildout. No risks surfaced that modify the thesis.

GNRC.headline: C&I segment inflected from +4.9% (FY25) to +28% (Q1 26) as data center demand ramps; overall revenue turned from -2% to +12%; Enercon acquisition and segment reorganization signal strategic pivot toward hyperscale custom power.

shifts.trend: C&I segment: +4.9% FY25 → +28% Q1 26; total revenue: -2% FY25 → +12% Q1 26
shifts.note: FY25 residential -6.8% on quiet hurricane season was the drag; Q1 residential +1% as C&I inflects — net turning positive

shifts.trend: Data center cited as primary C&I growth driver in both periods; large-megawatt diesel genset market 'significantly supply-constrained' (FY25 10-K); Enercon acquisition adds custom hyperscale power and industrial enclosures (Q1 26)
shifts.note: Segment reorganization from Domestic/International to Residential/C&I (Mar 2026) — reporting now aligned with the actual growth driver

shifts.trend: Adjusted EBITDA: $716M (17.0% margin, FY25) → $193M (18.2% margin, Q1 26, +120bps); C&I segment margin 11.4% FY25 → 13.0% Q1 26 (+160bps)
shifts.note: Residential margin improvement in Q1 (25.1% vs 20.3%) on operational efficiencies partly explains the gain

shifts.trend: Large-megawatt backup generator market: 'significantly supply-constrained' (FY25 10-K) → same constraint language implied by Enercon acquisition of custom power capabilities (Q1 26)
shifts.note: Supply constraint is a tailwind for pricing; Enercon gives GNRC capacity to serve hyperscale directly

GNRC.verdict: The data-center inflection in C&I is real and accelerating — from +4.9% to +28% in one quarter. Enercon and the segment reorganization confirm management is doubling down on hyperscale power as the strategic priority. Thesis strengthening; residential softness is the residual drag.

GOOGL.headline: Google Cloud growth jumped from +36% FY25 to +63% Q1 26 while Cloud op margin tripled YoY to 33%; capex annualizing above $140B vs $91.4B in FY25; RPO nearly doubled in one quarter ($242.8B → $467.6B) on multi-GW TPU supply agreements.

shifts.trend: Google Cloud: $58.7B (+36% FY25) → $20.0B (+63% Q1 26); Cloud op income: $13.9B (FY25, 24% margin) → $6.6B (Q1 26, 33% margin)
shifts.note: Cloud revenue re-accelerating while margins expanding simultaneously — most favorable combination of any hyperscaler reported

shifts.trend: Capex: $52.5B (FY24) → $91.4B (FY25) → $35.7B Q1 26 alone (annualizing $142B+); mgmt: 2026 capex will 'significantly increase' vs 2025
shifts.note: RPO $242.8B at Dec 31 → $467.6B at Mar 31 — multi-GW TPU hardware supply agreements signed in Q1 drive the jump

shifts.trend: First-time TPU hardware supply agreement disclosed Q1 — 'multi-gigawatt' deals where revenue starts late 2026, majority 2027; includes credit backstops for third-party DC/power customers
shifts.note: Google is now both buyer and seller of AI compute — supplying TPU capacity to external customers who need on-premise or co-located compute

shifts.trend: Committed $40B to a private company (Mar 2026, likely OpenAI or major AI lab); $16B funded into Waymo Feb 2026; $29.5B Wiz + $5.9B Intersect acquisitions closed
shifts.note: Capital deployment concentrated in AI and security — FY26 balance sheet commitments very large

GOOGL.verdict: This is the strongest across-the-board quarter of any hyperscaler in this dataset: Cloud growing faster, margins expanding, RPO doubling. The TPU supply agreement disclosure is the key new data — Google is monetizing its AI chip advantage externally. Thesis strongly intact.

HPE.headline: Juniper integration is on track with Networking +151.5% in the first full quarter; AI servers growing but flagged as margin-dilutive; memory component shortages are a new supply risk added to Q1 but not in the FY25 10-K.

shifts.trend: Networking: +51.1% FY25 (5 months Juniper) → +151.5% Q1 FY26 (full quarter); total revenue +13.8% FY25 → +18.4% Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Comparisons are Juniper-distorted for 12 months post-close (July 2025); organic Network growth is the real KPI to watch

shifts.trend: Gross margin: improved on Juniper high-margin networking mix; non-GAAP operating margin 12.7% (+280bps) Q1; FY25 non-GAAP 9.8% (-0.7pts) on integration costs
shifts.note: AI server segment flagged as 'limited margins' due to GPU pass-through pricing — a structural constraint, not a temporary one

shifts.trend: Memory component shortages flagged as a NEW risk factor in Q1 FY26 — not present in FY25 10-K
shifts.note: Memory scarcity is pervasive across the AI infrastructure stack; HPE is now experiencing it on the server side

shifts.trend: $600M Juniper synergies by FY28 requiring $800M investment — commitment unchanged across both filings; ARR +63% FY25 on Juniper aaS ramp
shifts.note: Synergy timeline and investment unchanged — no integration surprise in either direction

HPE.verdict: Juniper integration is proceeding as planned and the networking revenue ramp is clear. The structural issue — AI servers carry thin margins because GPU cost flows through — limits how much AI server growth contributes to the bottom line. Net: networking thesis intact, server thesis is a volume story not a margin story.

HUBB.headline: Electrical Solutions organic growth accelerated from +7.1% (FY25) to +10.6% (Q1 26) on data center strength; Utility Solutions growing on T&D demand; backlog $2.16B expected to ship substantially all in 2026.

shifts.trend: Electrical Solutions: +7.1% organic FY25 → +10.6% organic Q1 26; total revenue: +3.8% FY25 → +11% Q1 26
shifts.note: Data center and light industrial explicitly cited as primary Electrical Solutions growth driver in both filings

shifts.trend: Utility Solutions: +2.0% (organic +1.1%) FY25 → +10% (organic +6.8%) Q1 26; T&D and substation strength cited in both periods
shifts.note: Utility segment accelerating adds diversification to the datacenter-driven Electrical story

shifts.trend: Adjusted operating margin: +80bps FY25 (22.5%) → Q1 26 overall +110bps; Electrical Solutions Q1 adjusted margin -30bps to 16.4% on commodity/tariff inflation
shifts.note: Utility Solutions margin expanded +190bps adjusted in Q1 — offsetting Electrical compression

shifts.trend: Backlog $2.16B at YE25 (+14% YoY, substantially all to ship in 2026); Q1 acceleration in backlog-filling as organic growth accelerated
shifts.note: Backlog provides 3-4 month revenue visibility; datacenter spend cycle supporting replenishment

HUBB.verdict: Steady compounder executing well on data center and T&D demand. Revenue and margin trajectory improving. No new risks surfaced. Hubbel is a differentiated beneficiary of the power infrastructure buildout without the volatility of hyperscaler-direct exposure.

INTC.headline: DCAI revenue inflected to +22% YoY in Q1 on premium server ASPs; Intel Foundry operating loss narrowed for a second period ($13.3B FY24 → $10.3B FY25 → $2.4B Q1 26); supply constraints cited as persistent through 2026 are both gating revenue and signaling underlying demand.

shifts.trend: DCAI revenue: $14.3B (FY25, +$794M YoY) → $5.1B (Q1 26, +22% YoY) on 27% higher server ASPs; server volume -5% due to supply constraints, not demand
shifts.note: Market demand exceeding available supply in Q1 — INTC explicitly stated this in 10-Q; supply-constrained growth is qualitatively different from demand-constrained

shifts.trend: Intel Foundry operating loss: -$13.3B (FY24) → -$10.3B (FY25) → -$2.4B (Q1 26 alone, not annualized); 18A high-volume production ramping, external revenue $174M
shifts.note: Foundry loss trajectory is improving but Q1 is a single quarter; $2.4B * 4 = $9.6B annualized still very large

shifts.trend: Intel 7 wafer supply constrained Q4 FY25 (FY25 10-K) → substrate/memory/component shortages expected throughout 2026, most severe H1 (Q1 10-Q)
shifts.note: Industry-wide substrate and memory shortage is limiting Intel's ability to fulfill server demand — a revenue headwind but also a demand-validation signal

shifts.trend: NVIDIA $5B equity investment + SoftBank $2B + US Gov 159M shares (FY25 10-K); Apollo Ireland SCIP buyout completing Q2 26 (Q1 10-Q) — partner capital reducing Intel's own investment burden
shifts.note: If Intel 14A fails to attract a significant external foundry customer, mgmt may pause/discontinue — stated both filings

INTC.verdict: Turnaround in progress but fragile. DCAI demand is real (supply-constrained, not demand-constrained). Foundry loss narrowing trajectory is the bull case. The risk is 18A yield/cost versus TSMC N2/N1.4 — if INTC can't win external foundry volume, the Foundry thesis breaks. Watch for 14A customer announcement.

KLAC.headline: Revenue accelerated from +7% to +11% on DRAM/HBM-led investment; Korea revenue surged +80% in Q3 vs +34% in Q2 as SK Hynix HBM capex ramps; China stabilized at 24% of revenue vs prior-quarter declines.

shifts.trend: Revenue: $3.30B (+7% Q2) → $3.42B (+11% Q3, record); Semiconductor Process Control: $3.00B (+9% Q2) → $3.08B (+13% Q3)
shifts.note: Q3 was record revenue — WFE momentum accelerating into memory cycle

shifts.trend: Korea revenue: +34% Q2 ($680M) → +80% Q3 ($681M, same dollars but faster YoY growth vs prior-year base); memory/DRAM-HBM-led cited both quarters
shifts.note: North America also strong (+38% Q2, +40% Q3) on domestic memory/advanced packaging investment

shifts.trend: China: 30.2% of Q2 revenue (-9% YoY) → 24.2% of Q3 ($830M, +5% YoY); nine-month China revenue essentially flat at $3.09B
shifts.note: China stabilizing after prior-year export-control headwinds; not growing but no longer dragging

shifts.trend: Gross margin: 61.4% (Q2) → 61.1% (Q3, -30bps) from DRAM chip costs in image computers; mgmt: 'transitory'
shifts.note: Services growing to 24% then 23% of revenue — recurring base providing stability; 17th consecutive annual dividend increase

KLAC.verdict: Thesis intact — KLAC is benefiting from both the HBM technology-transition capex (Korea) and advanced packaging/2nm-node spend (North America/Taiwan). GM compression from DRAM image-computer costs is small and mgmt-framed as transitory. Record revenue is the headline.

LEU.headline: Execution milestones accumulating rapidly: $900M HALEU DOE task order (Jan 26) → Fluor EPC contract (Feb 26) → Oklo JV exploration (Mar 26); backlog grew from $3.8B to $3.9B but contingent LEU still awaiting capacity build.

shifts.trend: Revenue: ~$300M FY25 annualized → $76.7M Q1 FY26 (+5% YoY); Technical Solutions +47% (HALEU contract); LEU segment -13% (SWU volume -47%, offset by price +52%)
shifts.note: Total revenue growth is modest; thesis is about backlog, regulatory milestones, and nuclear fuel scarcity, not near-term revenue inflection

shifts.trend: SWU spot price: $34 (2018 low) → $200 at Q1 26 end (+488% recovery); only company with active NRC HALEU enrichment license in US
shifts.note: Russian TENEX supplies 43% of global enrichment capacity; US non-Russian market structurally undersupplied

shifts.trend: FY25 10-K: $560M+ Oak Ridge centrifuge manufacturing investment announced; Q1 26: Fluor EPC contract signed for Piketon LEU+HALEU commercial expansion; Oklo JV for HALEU deconversion co-location explored
shifts.note: Capital commitment accelerating with each filing — three distinct expansion initiatives now in progress

shifts.trend: Jan 2026: $900M ACO HALEU task order awarded; Feb 2026: Fluor EPC contract signed; Mar 2026: Oklo JV announced; DOE Aurora Nuclear Safety Design Agreement parallel track
shifts.note: FY27 DOE budget does not include HALEU cascade funding — key legislative risk remains; contingent contracts ($2.4B) cannot convert without additional appropriations

LEU.verdict: Regulatory and partnership milestones are stacking up faster than revenue — which is appropriate for a pre-commercial nuclear enrichment company. The thesis depends on Congress funding the HALEU cascade; without it, $2.4B in contingent backlog stays contingent. Monitoring legislative appropriations is the key risk variable.

LITE.headline: Revenue accelerated from +65.5% to +90.1% and gross margin expanded from 36.1% to 44.2% — both metrics improving sharply as 200G-lane (1.6T building block) supply allocation tightens; LITE is the InP/EML laser chip choke point.

shifts.note: Both Components and Systems accelerating; cloud transceiver Systems +$137M in Q3 alone

shifts.trend: Gross margin: 36.1% (+1,130bps YoY in Q2) → 44.2% (+1,540bps YoY in Q3); absolute sequential expansion +810bps from Q2 to Q3
shifts.note: Margin expansion is structural, not cyclical — driven by supply allocation in a constrained market at rising 200G-lane ASPs

shifts.trend: Q2: 'demand is outpacing current supply which has led to decisions on supply allocation'; Q3: language hardened — 'has required us to make decisions on supply allocation'
shifts.note: The supply allocation language tightening over two quarters means LITE is actively choosing which customers to serve — a pricing power signal

shifts.trend: Top-2 customers: 24% + 17% (41% combined, Q2) → 26% + 12% (38% combined, Q3); top customer share rose while second customer share dropped
shifts.note: Total top-2 concentration easing (41% → 38%) but #1 growing — likely one hyperscaler taking a larger share of constrained supply

LITE.verdict: This is the most dynamic supply-constrained story in the optics stack — two consecutive quarters of accelerating revenue and expanding margins while supply allocation tightens. The 200G-lane laser chip bottleneck is real and LITE controls it. Watch for any capacity addition announcements that could signal easing.

LRCX.headline: Revenue inflected from flat QoQ to +9% QoQ as DRAM/HBM investment surged; memory mix rose from 34% to 39% of revenue; China held at 34-35% despite no further export-control headwinds being added.

shifts.trend: Revenue: $5.34B (Q2, flat QoQ) → $5.84B (Q3, +9% QoQ); 9-month $16.51B vs $13.26B prior year (+24%)
shifts.note: Systems revenue led +11% QoQ on DRAM customer investment surge — leading indicator of memory capex acceleration

shifts.trend: Memory mix: 34% (Q2) → 39% (Q3); Foundry: 59% → 54%; DRAM and NVM investment explicitly cited as Q3 driver
shifts.note: Korea revenue: 20% Q2 → 23% Q3 as SK Hynix HBM capex accelerates; Taiwan 23% both quarters

shifts.trend: China: 35% of Q2 revenue → 34% of Q3; no new export-control restrictions added between the two filings
shifts.note: China plateauing as a share of revenue rather than declining further — legacy node investment sustaining

shifts.trend: Gross margin: 49.6% (Q2) → 49.8% (Q3, +20bps); six-month 50.0% vs 47.7% prior year (+230bps)
shifts.note: Capital returns accelerating: $1.16B Q3 repurchases + $326M dividends + $751M debt paydown

LRCX.verdict: DRAM/HBM memory capex is the Q3 catalyst — LRCX is a direct beneficiary as etch tool leader in HBM stacking processes. The shift from flat to +9% QoQ is meaningful. China stabilizing (not declining) removes a prior headwind. Thesis intact with improving momentum.

META.headline: Revenue re-accelerated from +22% FY25 to +33% Q1 26; capex guidance raised from $115-135B to $125-145B within one quarter; non-cancelable commitments jumped to $237.7B — Meta is the most aggressive AI capex spender per quarter in the group.

shifts.trend: Revenue: $201.0B (+22% FY25) → $56.3B (+33% Q1 26); ad impressions +12% FY25 → +19% Q1 26; price per ad +9% → +12%
shifts.note: Both impressions and price per ad accelerating simultaneously — exceptional underlying ad market

shifts.trend: Capex: $72.2B (FY25) → guided $115-135B (Jan 2026) → raised to $125-145B (Apr 2026, within one quarter of initial guidance)
shifts.note: The guidance raise within a single quarter is unusual — signals AI infrastructure demand visibility is increasing, not decreasing

shifts.trend: Non-cancelable contractual commitments: not separately disclosed (FY25 10-K) → $237.7B at Mar 31 26 ($42.3B in 2026, $47.7B in 2027); off-balance-sheet leases not yet commenced $182.9B
shifts.note: April 2026 added another $24B in multi-year infrastructure contracts post-Q1 — commitment acceleration shows no sign of pausing

shifts.trend: FoA operating income $102.5B (FY25, 51% margin) → $22.9B Q1 26 (41% of revenue, down from implied ~51%); RL operating loss $19.2B FY25 → $4.03B Q1 26
shifts.note: Q1 margin compression appears seasonal/mix; RL losses running at FY25 pace — similar losses guided for full FY26

META.verdict: Meta is executing on both the core ad business (re-acceleration) and the AI infrastructure build (guidance raised immediately). The $237.7B in committed spend is the most visible capex signal of any company in this portfolio. Thesis strongly intact; RL losses are the structural drag to watch.

MOD.headline: Climate Solutions data center growth nearly doubled — $67M incremental Q2 to $130M incremental Q3 — while segment margin remained under pressure from capacity ramp costs; Gentherm RMT announced in Q3 reshapes MOD into a pure-play data center cooling company.

shifts.trend: Climate Solutions: +24% Q2 (data center +$67M) → +51% Q3 (data center +$130M); 9-month data center product sales up $221.7M YoY
shifts.note: Both North America and Europe hyperscale/colocation cited; demand geographically broad

shifts.trend: Climate Solutions segment operating margin: 17.6% (Q2 FY25 prior year comp) → 13.7% (Q2 FY26) → 15.3% (Q3 FY26); partial recovery but still well below prior levels
shifts.note: Margin pressure is explicitly from temporary ramp inefficiencies; $100M capex announced Q2 → incremental data center manufacturing lease signed Q3

shifts.trend: Performance Technologies -4% (Q2) and still dragging consolidated results in Q3; Gentherm RMT (Jan 2026) will remove PT from MOD's P&L when it closes end of CY26
shifts.note: Post-RMT, MOD will be essentially 100% Climate Solutions — a pure-play data center + commercial HVAC company

shifts.trend: Q2: $100M incremental capex announced over next 12 months; Q3: additional data center manufacturing lease signed; Gentherm transaction: $210M cash to MOD at close
shifts.note: Gentherm cash proceeds will fund further data center capacity expansion; net capital position improving

MOD.verdict: The data center growth acceleration is real ($67M → $130M incremental YoY), but margins won't recover until capacity ramp costs normalize. The Gentherm RMT is the strategic unlocking event — post-close, MOD is a clean-sheet pure-play cooling story with no PT drag. Thesis depends on margin recovery timing.

MRVL.headline: Data center grew from +38% Q3 to +46% FY26 and reached 74% of revenue; closed Celestial AI ($1.3B) and XConn ($280M) post-year-end; distributor concentration tightened from 34% to 37% — one hyperscaler effectively running the print.

shifts.trend: Data center: $1.52B (+38% YoY, 73% of Q3 mix) → $6.1B (+46% YoY, 74% of FY26 mix); total revenue +37% Q3 → +42% FY26
shifts.note: Custom AI ASICs and electro-optics are both growing fast; silicon photonics roadmap (5nm→3nm→2nm→1.4nm) explicitly disclosed in 10-K

shifts.trend: Distributor A (hyperscaler conduit): 34% of Q3 revenue → 37% of FY26; Customer A direct: 11% Q3 → 14% FY26
shifts.note: Celestial AI acquisition ($1.3B) positions MRVL as supplier of photonic fabric scale-up interconnect to the same hyperscaler network

shifts.trend: Gross margin: 51.6% (Q3, ex-prior-year restructuring items) → 51.0% (FY26, +9.7pts YoY vs impaired FY25 base); operating margin expanding on higher revenue
shifts.note: Celestial AI/XConn amortization will compress GM in FY27 — headwind already flagged

shifts.trend: Post-Q3: Celestial AI closed Feb 2 for ~$1.3B cash + 24.5M shares; XConn closed Feb 10 for $280M + 2.1M shares — two acquisitions in 10 days
shifts.note: Both acquisitions extend the custom AI ASIC platform into photonic fabric (Celestial) and PCIe/CXL switching (XConn); MRVL building the full AI interconnect stack

MRVL.verdict: Data center dominance is complete (74% of revenue, growing faster each period). Distributor concentration at 37% is the key risk — MRVL's near-term results are effectively one hyperscaler's capex decision. Celestial AI and XConn extend the addressable market long-term. Thesis intact with concentration risk the main watch.

MSFT.headline: Azure re-accelerated from +39% to +40%; commercial RPO held at $625-627B; capex $29.9B → $30.9B flat sequentially — the infrastructure spend level has plateaued but at an unprecedented absolute level.

shifts.trend: Azure: +39% (Q2) → +40% (Q3); Microsoft Cloud: $51.5B (+26%) → $54.5B (+29%); commercial RPO $625B → $627B (essentially flat)
shifts.note: Azure re-acceleration from 39% to 40% is incremental but directionally positive; cloud margin pressured both quarters

shifts.trend: Capex additions: $29.9B (Q2) → $30.9B (Q3); H1 total $49.3B; 9-month total $80.1B vs $40.9B prior year (+96%)
shifts.note: Capex has reached a high plateau — the Q2→Q3 flat trajectory suggests a spending ceiling may be forming, or spending will accelerate in Q4

shifts.trend: Microsoft Cloud gross margin 67% (Q2) — pressured by AI infrastructure; Q3 total revenue margin flat as cloud infra investment offsets AI services revenue
shifts.note: OpenAI recapitalization dilution gain $5.9B (9M non-operating) — unusual item, not recurring

shifts.trend: OpenAI partnership extended (Oct 2025 new agreement); CRWV Q1 filing confirms Microsoft is 45% of CRWV revenue — MSFT as hyperscaler is heavily committed to neocloud GPU supply
shifts.note: MSFT is the primary off-taker for CRWV (45% of $2.08B Q1 revenue) — significant implied GPU/AI infrastructure commitment beyond what MSFT's own 10-Q discloses

MSFT.verdict: Azure re-acceleration is confirmed, RPO is massive, and capex is holding at unprecedented levels. The lack of a big beat or guidance raise makes this a steady-state story — MSFT is executing within expectations. Not a new catalyst, but no thesis-breaking developments either.

MXL.headline: Revenue growth accelerated from +30% to +43%; Infrastructure segment (optical DSPs, wireless backhaul) is replacing Broadband as the primary growth driver; customer concentration is broadening as the 1.6T cycle begins.

shifts.trend: Revenue: $467.6M (+30% FY25) → $137.2M (+43% Q1 26); Infrastructure: +$34.3M FY25 → +$36.3M Q1 26 as lead segment
shifts.note: Broadband was the FY25 driver (+$87.6M, +59%); Infrastructure is taking over in Q1 — the 800G→1.6T optical transition is shifting the demand mix

shifts.trend: Top-10 customers: 65% of FY25 revenue → 56% Q1 26; top customer: not quantified FY25 (>20%) → 13% Q1 26
shifts.note: Broadening customer base as optical DSP adoption spreads across transceiver makers for the 1.6T cycle

shifts.trend: Gross margin: 54% (pre-FY25 trough) → 57% (FY25, +3pts) on mix and lower intangible amortization
shifts.note: R&D -7% in FY25 on workforce reductions; mgmt noted R&D needs to re-expand for next-gen products — cost will rise

shifts.trend: 10-K explicitly names 400G→800G→1.6T transition as the optical DSP roadmap; Q1 Infrastructure growth confirms MXL is shipping into this transition
shifts.note: Connectivity (MoCA/ethernet) seasonal Q1 decline is normal — infrastructure growth is what matters

MXL.verdict: MXL is recovering from its FY24 inventory correction trough and the 1.6T optical cycle is providing the next leg of growth via Infrastructure. Customer broadening is positive for earnings quality. The main risk is execution of the next-gen optical DSP R&D that mgmt acknowledged needs investment.

NOW.headline: Consistent 21-22% revenue growth across two periods; RPO/cRPO growth rates holding at 23-27%; Now Assist AI agent monetization active but consumption-model uncertainty remains the key open question.

shifts.note: Growth rate is remarkably stable — no acceleration or deceleration despite AI agent narratives

shifts.trend: RPO: $28.2B (+27% FY25) → $27.7B (+25% Q1 26); cRPO: +25% FY25 → +23% Q1 26; slight deceleration in both metrics
shifts.note: Normal Q1 seasonality in cRPO (fewer large deals close in January); trend is stable rather than deteriorating

shifts.trend: Now Assist deeply embedded across ITSM/CSM/HRSD/SecOps in FY25; Q1 confirms 'subscription revenue to increase in absolute dollars'; no explicit Agentforce-type acceleration language
shifts.note: Consumption-based AI agent pricing is new and uncertain per FY25 10-K — could accelerate revenue or create lumpiness

shifts.trend: Self-hosted upfront revenue $492M (FY25) → $138M Q1 26 (vs $157M prior year Q1); US federal on-prem demand driving self-hosted mix
shifts.note: Federal on-prem deployment signals are consistent across both filings

NOW.verdict: Reliable 20%+ SaaS compounder with AI agent optionality (Now Assist). No acceleration evident yet from Agentforce-equivalent monetization. Thesis intact but the bull case requires evidence that consumption pricing unlocks faster growth in future quarters.

NVT.headline: Systems Protection segment jumped from +42.2% FY25 to +76.1% Q1 26; organic growth accelerated sharply from 13% to 34% as data center enclosures and liquid cooling volume surged; margins expanding despite tariff headwinds.

shifts.trend: Total revenue: +29.5% FY25 → +53.5% Q1 26; Systems Protection: +42.2% FY25 → +76.1% Q1 26; organic growth: +12.6% FY25 → +34.4% Q1 26
shifts.note: Infrastructure (data centers) contributed ~16pts organic growth FY25 → ~46pts Q1 26 — data center driving the organic acceleration

shifts.trend: Systems Protection segment margin: 20.7% (FY25) → 22.7% (Q1 26, +200bps) on operating leverage from volume surge
shifts.note: Gross margin 37.7% FY25 → 35.9% Q1 26 (-290bps) on tariffs/raw material/mix — segment margin expanding while consolidated GM contracts

shifts.trend: Avail EPG acquisition $1.0B (May 2025) added enclosures capacity; Thermal Management divested to Brookfield $1.6B (Jan 2025); capex FY25 undisclosed but FY26 stepping up
shifts.note: Portfolio reshaping: sold thermal, acquired enclosures — bet on enclosures and liquid cooling, not heat exchangers

shifts.trend: Backlog $2.3B at YE25 driven by EPG acquisition and data center growth; both filings emphasize 'AI/data center megatrends to continue driving Systems Protection sales growth'
shifts.note: No language suggesting backlog drawdown — demand replenishing faster than shipments

NVT.verdict: NVT is executing cleanly on the data center enclosures/liquid cooling megatrend — organic growth tripling in one period is rare and real. The portfolio reshaping (thermal out, enclosures in) was prescient. Watch consolidated GM: tariff/raw material pressure is real even as segment margins expand.

OKLO.headline: Between the two filings: order book expanded to 18,100 MWe, DOE HALEU task order won ($900M), Meta prepayment agreement signed for 1.2 GW, Aurora Safety Design Agreement approved — four material milestones in five months.

shifts.trend: Order book: ~13,350 MWe (Q3 25 start of quarter) → ~18,100 MWe (Q3 25 end) → Meta 1.2 GW prepayment signed Jan 2026 (converting LOI to committed capital); $1.68B Tennessee Fuel Center roadmap added
shifts.note: Meta is the first hyperscaler to prepay (not just LOI) — validates commercial model

shifts.trend: Q3 FY25: $968M raised 9M financing; FY25 10-K: $300M additional ATM raised; FY26 investing activities guided $350-450M; Tennessee Fuel Center up to $1.68B multi-year
shifts.note: Cash burn ramping: FY25 operating cash $65-80M → FY26 guided $80-100M; investing $350-450M FY26 vs $605M Q3-9M implies annual run-rate increasing

shifts.trend: Q3: NRC Phase I pre-application with no gaps; PDC topical report accepted Sep 2025; Q3: DOE Reactor Pilot Program 3 projects selected; 10-K: Aurora Nuclear Safety Design Agreement approved Feb 2026
shifts.note: Regulatory approvals accumulating faster than typical nuclear projects; DOE partnership is strategic credibility

shifts.trend: Q3: Switch 12 GW master, Equinix/Diamondback/Prometheus LOIs; 10-K: Meta 1.2 GW prepayment, Switch still in, Tennessee Fuel Center added as non-hyperscaler customer (DOE)
shifts.note: Still a very concentrated pre-revenue company but customer type diversifying beyond just hyperscalers

OKLO.verdict: Material milestone accumulation between filings — this is the inflection story. Meta prepayment is the first real cash signal that the commercial model works. Regulatory pace is ahead of typical nuclear timelines. The risk is execution: first Aurora powerhouse deployment target 2028 is still 2 years away.

ORCL.headline: OCI cloud infrastructure accelerated from +68% to +84%; RPO grew from $523.3B to $552.6B; 9-month capex $39.2B (3x prior year pace) as Oracle races to match AWS/MSFT capacity; debt stack ballooned with $47.7B in notes issued.

shifts.trend: OCI cloud infrastructure: +68% Q2 ($4.08B) → +84% Q3 ($4.89B); total cloud revenue +21% → +22%; total revenue +14% → +22%
shifts.note: Total revenue growth re-accelerated sharply Q2→Q3; OCI is the fastest-growing cloud infrastructure segment in the group

shifts.trend: RPO: $97.3B (Nov 2024) → $523.3B (Nov 2025) → $552.6B (Feb 2026); H1 growth was the inflection; Q3 growing but at slower pace vs the initial surge
shifts.note: RPO growth rate is moderating from the initial contract-signing surge; still growing sequentially

shifts.trend: Capex: H1 $20.5B (Q2 filing) → 9M $39.2B (Q3 filing, = $18.7B in Q3 alone); trend 'to continue next few fiscal years'
shifts.note: 9M run-rate implies $52B+ annualized FY26 capex vs $12.1B in prior year H1 — a 4x step-up underway

shifts.trend: $17.9B senior notes issued Sep 2025; $24.8B more in Feb 2026; $5B mandatory convertible preferred; ATM program initiated — total new capital raised $47.7B in 9 months
shifts.note: ORCL is funding its capex entirely with debt and equity issuance; cash flow from operations not keeping pace

ORCL.verdict: OCI growth is the fastest in the group and accelerating — the thesis is intact. The leverage trajectory is the key risk: $47.7B in new debt/equity issued in 9 months to fund a 4x capex step-up. If RPO converts to revenue fast enough, the math works. If not, the balance sheet is the constraint.

PLTR.headline: Revenue accelerated from +53% (FY25) to +85% (Q1 26); operating margin jumped from an average ~31% FY25 to 46% Q1 26; commercial revenue growth (95%) is now outpacing government (76%) for the first time.

shifts.trend: Revenue: $4.5B (+53%/+56% gov/comm FY25) → $1.63B (+85% Q1 26); commercial: +60% FY25 → +95% Q1 26; government: +53% FY25 → +76% Q1 26
shifts.note: Both segments accelerating but commercial growing faster — AIP bootcamp model driving enterprise wins at high velocity

shifts.trend: Operating margin: ~31% FY25 average → 46% Q1 26; gross margin: 80% Q1 25 → 87% Q1 26 (+700bps); contribution margin: 66% FY25 → 73% Q1 26
shifts.note: Operating leverage is compounding: revenue growing faster than costs at every line item

shifts.trend: No customer >10% in Q1 26 or Q1 25; US revenue 79% of Q1 26 (vs 71% Q1 25) — US concentration increasing but customer-level diversification improving
shifts.note: US commercial penetration via AIP bootcamps means more customers at smaller sizes; diversification at the customer level even as geographic concentration in US grows

shifts.trend: Government contribution margin: 62% FY25 → 73% Q1 26; commercial contribution margin: 61% FY25 → 73% Q1 26; both improving simultaneously
shifts.note: Software-native operating leverage materializing — contribution margins at 73% are unusually high

PLTR.verdict: PLTR is one of the fastest-improving financial profiles in the dataset — both revenue and margins accelerating simultaneously. The 85% revenue growth rate at $1.6B quarterly scale is exceptional. The bear case (no customer >10%, US-concentrated) is not emerging as a constraint. Thesis strongly intact.

PWR.headline: Revenue growth accelerated from +20.3% to +26%; backlog jumped from $43.98B to $48.47B in one quarter (+10% QoQ); RPO growing 10% sequentially — data-center-driven electric utility and underground infrastructure demand accelerating.

shifts.trend: Revenue: $28.5B (+20.3% FY25) → $7.87B (+26.3% Q1 26); Electric segment: +21% FY25 → core Q1 acceleration on data center utility demand
shifts.note: Q1 operating income +41.7% outpacing revenue growth — operating leverage evident
Backlog growing faster than revenue — work orders coming in faster than PWR can complete them; no sign of deceleration

shifts.trend: $3.30B deployed on acquisitions in FY25 including DSI (mechanical/plumbing for data center load centers); DSI and CEI both now contributing to Q1 growth
shifts.note: DSI expands addressable market from electric to mechanical/plumbing infrastructure inside data centers — M&A extending moat

shifts.trend: Gross margin: 13.4% (FY25 implied) → 14.1% (Q1 26); operating margin 3.8% → 4.3%; Underground margin expanded from 5.7% to 7.3% FY25
shifts.note: PWR operates on thin margins structurally (E&C model) but Q1 shows expansion trend

PWR.verdict: PWR is the clearest beneficiary of data-center-driven electric grid buildout — backlog/RPO growing 10% in a single quarter is unusual. DSI/CEI acquisitions expand the inside-DC infrastructure story. Thesis strongly intact; the main risk is execution speed (skilled labor) as backlogs extend.

SNOW.headline: Full-year FY26 confirms +31% product revenue growth vs +29% Q3 pace; NRR held at 125% (vs 126% Q3); RPO grew from $7.9B to $9.8B; $1M+ customer cohort grew 27% — large enterprise demand is the story.

shifts.trend: Product revenue: $1.16B (+29% Q3 FY26) → $4.5B implied full FY26 (+31%, acceleration); total customers +21% to 13,328; $1M+ customers +27% to 733
shifts.note: The $1M+ cohort growing faster (27%) than total customers (21%) — land and expand working at the high end

shifts.trend: Forbes Global 2000: 790 customers = ~43% of revenue (FY26); top cohort growing but not concentrating
shifts.note: NRR 126% → 125% — minor moderation, still exceptional; mgmt expects long-term NRR decline as base matures

shifts.trend: RPO: $7.9B (Oct 31 2025) → $9.8B (Jan 31 2026, +24% in one quarter); 46% to recognize next 12 months (vs 48% in Q3) — mix shifting slightly longer-dated
shifts.note: RPO growth rate (24% in one quarter) implies significant new contract signings in the fiscal Q4 period

shifts.trend: Net loss $1.3B FY26; stock-based comp ~34% of revenue (down from 41% FY25); operating cash flow $1.2B FY26
shifts.note: SBC declining as % of revenue is the margin path — consumption revenue model means COGS can scale efficiently

SNOW.verdict: FY26 confirms the Q3 trend and adds the $1M+ cohort acceleration as new evidence. The story is intact: cloud data platform growing ~30% with exceptional NRR. The uncertainty is consumption-model timing — revenue follows actual workload usage, not contract signing dates. No new risks surfaced.

SNPS.headline: Ansys full-quarter contribution inflated Q1 FY26 growth to +66% vs +15% FY25; organic Design Automation growing but Design IP -6% and China declining; $3.5B term loan repaid in Q1 — debt paydown is now the priority.

shifts.trend: Revenue: $7.05B (+15% FY25, $756M Ansys contribution) → $2.41B (+66% Q1 FY26, $885.6M Ansys); organic Synopsys growth was the FY25 -22% China-impacted number; Q1 organic vs prior-year ex-Ansys more modest
shifts.note: Comparisons will be Ansys-distorted for 12 months; FY25 vs FY24 organic was ex-Ansys and China-depressed

shifts.trend: China -22% ex-Ansys (FY25) on BIS restrictions; Q1 FY26 guidance: 'challenging near-term environment in China due to macro and Trade Restrictions'; Design IP segment -6% (China exposure)
shifts.note: May-July 2025 BIS 'is-informed' letter chaos removed; but underlying export-control risk persists for EDA to Chinese chip design

shifts.trend: $13.5B total debt at FY25-end → $3.5B term loan paid off in Q1 FY26; remaining: $10B senior notes + reduced term; buyback suspended until debt manageable
shifts.note: $34.9B total Ansys purchase price; debt is being aggressively paid down — signal of prioritizing balance sheet health over shareholder returns

shifts.trend: Design IP: muted/negative growth expected FY26 (mgmt guidance); Design Automation +96% (Ansys-inflated); Processor IP sold to GlobalFoundries Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Divestitures and Ansys integration are reshaping the portfolio toward highest-growth EDA tools; Design IP weakness is not new — persisted across both filings

SNPS.verdict: Ansys integration is absorbing capital and attention — debt paydown priority over buyback is the right short-term call. The organic EDA business is healthy (AI chip design cycles drive tool demand). China and Design IP are the soft spots. Once debt normalizes, the Ansys optionality becomes the bull case.

VRT.headline: Revenue accelerated from +27.7% to +30%; Americas organic growth jumped from ~21% (FY25) to 53% with margin expanding to 27.0%; gross margin expanded 400bps in Q1; backlog doubled to $15.0B and is still growing.

shifts.note: Americas organic growth is the primary driver; EMEA -20% Q1 is a drag — Americas more than offsets

shifts.trend: Gross margin: 36.3% (-30bps FY25) → 37.7% (Q1 26, +400bps); Americas segment margin: 26.8% (FY25) → 27.0% (Q1 26); operating profit +51.4%
shifts.note: FY25 GM was slightly compressed by tariffs; Q1 26 shows strong expansion — operating leverage materializing

shifts.trend: Combined order backlog: $7.2B (YE24) → $15.0B (YE25) → still growing in Q1 26; FY25 10-K: majority expected to ship in next 12-18 months
shifts.note: 'Very robust' AI/HPC demand language identical across both filings; hybrid air-and-liquid and HVDC architectures cited as new architectures driving incremental demand

shifts.trend: Capex: $226.4M (FY25) → guided $425-525M FY26 (reaffirmed in Q1 at same level); Q1 actual $114M puts FY26 on track for high end of range
shifts.note: PurgeRite acquisition in Q1 expanding portfolio; contingent consideration drove $33.2M corporate expense — integration cost real but modest

VRT.verdict: VRT is executing at the top of the cooling stack — backlog doubled, margins expanding, Americas segment dominating. The Q1 print confirms the FY25 thesis and adds gross margin expansion as new evidence of pricing power. EMEA softness is a watch item but not thesis-threatening.
